description: This figure displays engineering stress-strain curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels obtained from uniaxial tensile testing at ambient temperature and strain rate of 1.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹. The curves are organized into multiple sub-panels comparing different alloying series: carbon content variation (C-0.6 to C-1.5 wt%), manganese content variation (Mn-20 to Mn-35 wt%), and aluminum content variation (Al-6 to Al-12 wt%) under solution treatment at 1050°C, plus an additional panel showing Al-series treated at 1100°C. Each curve exhibits distinct yield points, work hardening characteristics, and fracture strains. Notable trends visible include that increasing C and Al content significantly elevates yield strength (from 401 MPa to 1125 MPa) and ultimate tensile strength (826–1191 MPa) while reducing uniform elongation (22–74%), whereas increasing Mn content decreases yield strength but maintains high ductility. The figure demonstrates that nano-sized κ-carbides, whose size increases with C/Al but decreases with Mn, strengthen the matrix via a shearing mechanism, with critical transitions in work hardening behavior and impact toughness occurring when carbide sizes exceed approximately 4 nm.

The figure presents technical engineering drawings of mechanical test specimen geometries used for characterizing Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the tensile specimen featuring a cylindrical gauge section with Ø5 mm diameter and 30 mm gauge length, 65 mm total length between fillets, M12 threaded gripping ends, R5 mm transition fillet radii, and a 60° conical pilot hole with 1 mm depth at the specimen axis. Subfigure (b) depicts the Charpy V-notch impact specimen with dimensions 55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm and a 2 mm deep V-notch. According to the caption and citation context, these geometries were employed for uniaxial tensile testing conducted at ambient temperature with an initial strain rate of 1.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ using an MTS machine, and for Charpy impact toughness measurements performed at −40 °C with the V-notch oriented perpendicular to the rod axis. The schematic specifies critical dimensional parameters including gauge diameter, fillet radii, and notch geometry essential for reproducible mechanical property evaluation across the alloy series (C-0.6 to C-1.5, Mn-20 to Mn-35, Al-6 to Al-12).

This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels subjected to solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 hours, illustrating the effects of varying alloying content on tensile mechanical properties. Subfigure (a) displays curves for Fe30Mn11AlxC steels with carbon contents ranging from 0.6C to 1.5C (black, red, green, and blue curves respectively), revealing a significant increase in yield strength from 542 MPa to 1125 MPa as carbon increases, accompanied by a transition from pronounced work hardening behavior in 0.6C steel to rapid work softening in 1.5C steel and reduced elongation from approximately 60% to 20%. Subfigure (b) shows curves for FexMn11Al1C steels with manganese contents varying from 20Mn to 34Mn (black, red, green, and blue curves), demonstrating an opposite trend where increasing Mn content decreases yield strength from 896 MPa to 401 MPa while improving total elongation from 28.5% to approximately 75% and enhancing work hardening rate. The caption notes that stress-strain jumps after yielding are artifacts from extensometer removal and do not affect mechanical measurements.

The figure displays engineering stress-strain curves for Fe30MnxAl1C low-density steels investigating the effect of aluminum content (6Al, 8Al, 10Al, 11Al, and 12Al in mass%) under two solution treatment conditions. Subfigure (c) shows alloys treated at 1050°C, where increasing Al content from 6% to 12% progressively raises yield strength from approximately 433 MPa to 846 MPa while reducing ductility; notably, the 12Al alloy exhibits premature fracture near 40% engineering strain. Subfigure (d) presents the same compositions treated at 1100°C, demonstrating significantly improved elongation for high-Al alloys, with the 12Al sample now sustaining over 60% strain. Both plots exhibit characteristic twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) behavior with continuous work hardening and ultimate tensile strengths ranging from 826 MPa to 967 MPa. The comparison establishes that elevating solution treatment temperature from 1050°C to 1100°C mitigates the strength-ductility trade-off in Al-rich compositions, attributed to grain coarsening and modified κ-carbide precipitation kinetics.

This figure presents the microstructure evolution of Fe30Mn11Al(x)C low-density steels with varying carbon contents (0.6%, 1.0%, 1.2%, and 1.5%) following solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 hours. Panels (a)-(d) display EBSD orientation maps revealing high-angle grain boundaries (black lines, θ>15°), annealing twin boundaries (Σ3, red lines), and BCC phase distribution (green regions in (a)). The 0.6%C alloy exhibits a dual-phase structure comprising approximately 30% BCC ferrite (average grain size 5.5 μm) and FCC austenite (7.3 μm), while higher carbon contents (1.0-1.5%) show single-phase austenite with significantly coarser grains (>22 μm). The twin boundary fraction decreases from approximately 60% to 50% with increasing carbon content. Panels (e)-(h) show dark-field TEM images of κ-carbide precipitation within the austenite matrix, with insets displaying selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along the [001]γ zone axis confirming the (001)κ||(001)γ orientation relationship. The κ-carbides coarsen markedly from sub-1 nm (0.6%C) to approximately 2 nm (1.0%C), 25 nm (1.2%C), and 80 nm (1.5%C), with the highest carbon content exhibiting distinct rectangular morphology indicating confined growth along {011} planes. These observations demonstrate that carbon content critically controls phase stability, grain refinement via ferrite pinning, and κ-carbide precipitation kinetics.

Figure 4 presents the microstructural evolution of Fe(x)Mn11Al1C low-density steels with varying Mn content (20%, 25%, 30%, and 35%) after solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h. Panels (a–d) show EBSD grain boundary maps where black lines denote high-angle boundaries (θ>15°), red lines indicate Σ3 twin boundaries, and green areas represent BCC ferrite phase. The images reveal that ferrite volume fraction decreases from 3.3% at 20% Mn to 0.65% at 25% Mn, disappearing completely at 30% and 35% Mn, while austenite grain size increases from 9.9 μm to 33 μm. Twin boundary fraction remains approximately 60% across samples. Panels (e–h) display dark-field TEM images (with corresponding SAED patterns in insets) showing κ-carbide precipitates imaged along [001]γ using κ-carbide reflections. The κ-carbide size decreases significantly with increasing Mn content: from approximately 18 nm at 20% Mn to 5 nm, 3 nm, and 2 nm at 25%, 30%, and 35% Mn, respectively. The SAED patterns confirm the coherent orientation relationship (001)κ||(001)γ. These results demonstrate that increasing Mn content promotes complete austenitization, grain coarsening, and simultaneous refinement of nano-scale κ-carbides.

Figure 5 presents the microstructural evolution of Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low-density steels with varying Al content (8%, 10%, 11%, and 12%) following solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 hours. Panels (a–d) display EBSD orientation maps (inverse pole figure or boundary maps) revealing high-angle grain boundaries (black lines, θ>15°), annealing twin boundaries (red lines, Σ3), and BCC phase distribution (green regions). The 8–11% Al alloys exhibit single-phase austenite with average grain sizes of approximately 28.3 μm, 29.7 μm, and 23.7 μm, respectively, whereas the 12% Al alloy (d) shows significantly refined austenite grains (12.6 μm) containing 3.1% BCC ferrite phase. Panels (e–h) provide transmission electron microscopy evidence of κ-carbide precipitation. The 8% Al sample (e) shows a bright-field TEM image with an inset selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along the [001]γ zone axis, confirming the absence of κ-carbides. Dark-field TEM images (f–h) taken using κ-carbide diffraction spots reveal uniformly distributed nanoscale precipitates (white contrast) in 10–12% Al steels, with average diameters increasing from approximately 1 nm (10% Al) to 1.5 nm (11% Al) and 15 nm (12% Al). These observations demonstrate that increasing Al content promotes κ-carbide formation and coarsening, while the presence of ferrite at 12% Al induces substantial grain refinement.

This figure presents quantitative assessments of alloying effects on microstructure and physical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels after solution heat treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by water quenching. Panel (a) displays the effect of alloying content on the average κ-carbide diameter (d_Car) plotted against element content in mass percent. The graph shows that both carbon (represented as 10×C%, red squares) and aluminum (Al%, white stars) strongly promote κ-carbide coarsening, with diameters increasing sharply from approximately 1 nm to 50 nm for C and from 0.5 nm to 15 nm for Al. In contrast, manganese (Mn%, blue circles) exhibits a weak inverse relationship, where increasing Mn content from 25% to 35% reduces carbide size from approximately 5 nm to 2 nm. Panel (b) illustrates the linear dependence of mass density (ρ) on alloying content, with the empirical relationship ρ = 8.1 - 0.102C% - 0.1Al% - 0.01Mn% (g/cm³). While all three elements reduce density, aluminum has the strongest effect (steepest slope), followed by carbon and manganese. These data collectively establish that C and Al are the critical elements controlling κ-carbide precipitation kinetics and size, whereas density follows predictable linear mixing behavior based on elemental mass percentages.

This figure presents the quantitative relationship between κ-carbide size (d_Car) and mechanical properties in solution-treated Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels. Subplot (a) displays yield strength (Rp0.2) and tensile strength (Rm) versus the square root of carbide diameter (d_Car^0.5), exhibiting linear increases described by Rp0.2 = 478 + 97.1d_Car^0.5 and Rm = 786 + 54.6d_Car^0.5 (units in MPa), indicating particle shear strengthening. Subplots (b) and (c) show total tensile elongation (A5) and area reduction (Z) decreasing linearly with d_Car^0.5 following A5 = 64.5 − 6.445d_Car^0.5 and Z = 78 − 3.85d_Car^0.5 (units in %), reflecting reduced work hardening and ductility. Subplot (d) presents Charpy impact toughness at −40°C (Akv), which drops precipitously from over 350 J to near zero as d_Car^0.5 exceeds approximately 2 nm^0.5. Collectively, these correlations demonstrate that while κ-carbide strengthening follows a shear mechanism, carbide coarsening severely compromises both uniform elongation and low-temperature toughness, establishing critical microstructural design limits for high-performance low-density steels.

This figure illustrates the work hardening behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels through true tensile stress-strain curves (solid lines) and corresponding work hardening rate curves (dashed lines) plotted against true strain. Panel (a) shows the effect of carbon content (0.6C, 1.0C, 1.2C, 1.5C) after 1050°C solution treatment, where decreasing carbon increases the work hardening rate to a maximum of approximately 1900 MPa for the 0.6C alloy. Panel (b) examines manganese content (20Mn, 25Mn, 30Mn, 35Mn) at 1050°C, demonstrating that higher Mn content elevates the work hardening rate up to ~2300 MPa for 35Mn steel. Panels (c) and (d) display aluminum content effects (6Al through 12Al) at 1050°C and 1100°C respectively, showing the work hardening rate increases with decreasing Al content, reaching ~2100 MPa for 6Al steel. Notably, the 0.6C and 30Mn steels exhibit monotonically decreasing work hardening rates, while other compositions display curves with initial rapid decline followed by a slow rise and subsequent second decline. The data reveal that true ultimate tensile stress (~1350 MPa) and true fracture stress (~2150 MPa) remain composition-independent, whereas work hardening behavior strongly correlates with κ-carbide precipitation. The figure supports the conclusion that high work hardening capacity, associated with fine κ-carbide dispersions, enables enhanced ductility through particle shear mechanisms.
